metrics.narrative: Louise Nichol articulated qualitative pain on the 4 June discovery call: failed GEP rollout with low adoption, users reverting to Excel and email, and 'lack of confidence' in the current tool. She described GEP as a 'lift-and-shift from the US with no mandate'. However, no quantified business impact has been captured: no cost of manual workarounds, cycle time, rogue spend, compliance risk, or savings opportunity. Louise stated the buyer is 'willing to pay for a solution that works', but without a quantified ROI case tied to adoption failure and hidden costs of non-compliance, Zycus cannot justify incremental spend vs the free GEP baseline. The RFI response must lead with adoption ROI and cost-of-inaction quantification to overcome the free-vs-paid gap.

competition.narrative: The primary competitor is GEP Worldwide, already deployed at JELD-WEN Europe and provided free by the US parent. On the 4 June call, Louise Nichol stated 'GEP costs are already in budget and will continue to be paid', meaning Zycus must justify incremental spend vs zero marginal cost to the European team. The competitive wedge is adoption failure: GEP's rollout was a 'lift-and-shift from the US with no mandate', resulting in low adoption, users reverting to Excel/email, and 'lack of confidence'. Louise stated the buyer is 'willing to pay for a solution that works', signaling openness to cost if Zycus can prove adoption credibility. How we win: lead RFI response with European customer adoption case studies, mandate-driven rollout plan, embedded change management in SOW, and quantified ROI showing the hidden cost of GEP non-adoption (rogue spend, manual work, compliance risk, cycle time). Make adoption the centerpiece of every deliverable to differentiate from GEP's failed execution.

flags: Win strategy: Lead the adoption and change-management conversation explicitly. The buyer's GEP pain is operational (low adoption, no mandate, users reverting to Excel), not technical. Zycus must position as the anti-GEP: mandate-driven rollout, embedded change management, European success stories with adoption metrics, and executive sponsorship from Day 1. Make adoption the wedge in every deliverable (pricing proposal, case studies, implementation plan). GEP failed on execution, not features—own the execution story.

Multi-thread immediately: only 1 buyer-side contact role (Louise Nichol, Evaluator) after two calls and 15 days. The deal is single-threaded to a project lead with no budget authority. Map and engage the VP Procurement Europe (account contact list shows candidate), the CFO or finance decision-maker (Dan Krinshaw or Lisa Oxnard per account contacts), and IT stakeholders (Dominic Gaffey, IT Director, on account). Ask Louise directly on the next call: 'Who will approve the budget and sign the contract?' and 'Who else should be in the room for the detailed demos?' Add confirmed buyer-side stakeholders as Contact Roles immediately.

Quantify the business case now: Louise has articulated clear pain (low adoption, manual workarounds, lack of confidence in GEP) but no hard ROI, savings target, headcount reduction, or cycle-time improvement. The next pricing/scoping conversation must extract a number: 'What would success look like in Year 1? How many procurement FTEs are working around GEP today? What is the cost of contract leakage or non-compliance risk?' Without a quantified case, the buyer cannot defend a switch away from free GEP to senior finance, and Zycus cannot build a compelling ROI story. Make this the centerpiece of the next call.

Map and engage Economic Buyer within 14 days. Louise Nichol is the evaluator but not the budget authority. Without EB identification and access, the deal will stall at RFI and cannot progress to commercials. Ask Louise on the next call who controls the budget for a Europe-wide S2C platform (likely CFO Dan Krinshaw or VP Procurement Europe) and request an intro. Make EB mapping the rank-1 action.

Multi-thread to senior stakeholders (procurement, finance, IT leadership) within 21 days. Only one buyer-side contact role (Louise). Single-threaded deals are fragile: if Louise loses internal support or leaves, the deal has no other advocate. Use the RFI response call to ask Louise who else should review the proposal (her manager, the European procurement lead, finance partner, IT sponsor for integration). Add them as contact roles and invite them to the next demo or workshop.

Lead with adoption credibility in every deliverable to overcome GEP failure scar and justify cost vs free.
